title: Chapter 1 Quiz: The History of IT Ethics
description: Ten questions covering the five information technology revolutions and the recurring ethical patterns they reveal.

questions.question: The chapter opens with the claim that every information technology revolution made two promises. Which pair best captures them?
options: Speed and accuracy
Liberation and control
Access and affordability
Innovation and stability
questions.explanation: The subtitle of the chapter states it directly: 'Every information revolution promised liberation. Every one enabled new forms of control. Both promises were kept.' This tension — that the same technology expands freedom and enables oppression — is the chapter's central thesis.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly lists all five IT revolutions covered in the chapter, in order?
options: Writing → Printing press → Telegraph → Mass media → World Wide Web
Writing → Alphabet → Printing press → Telephone → Internet
Cuneiform → Moveable type → Radio → Television → Social media
Writing → Printing press → Telephone → Cinema → Computers
questions.explanation: The chapter traces five revolutions: (1) Writing (c. 3200 BCE), (2) the Printing Press (c. 1440), (3) Distance Communication — telegraph and telephone (1830s onward), (4) Mass Media — radio and television (early 20th century), and (5) the World Wide Web (1990s). Each follows the liberation-and-control pattern.

questions.question: In Plato's dialogue Phaedrus, the Egyptian king Thamus rejects the god Theuth's gift of writing. What is Thamus's main objection?
options: Writing will allow rulers to forge laws and deceive the public
Written texts are too expensive for ordinary citizens to own
Writing will weaken memory and give people the illusion of knowledge without genuine understanding
Writing will destroy oral poetry and the cultural traditions it preserves
questions.explanation: Thamus argues that writing is a false remedy: it will produce forgetfulness in students' minds because they will rely on written marks rather than their own memory. They will 'seem to know' much but actually understand little — the illusion of wisdom without its substance. This is one of the earliest recorded arguments about the cognitive costs of new information technology.

questions.question: What made the printing press (c. 1440) ethically revolutionary, rather than merely technically useful?
options: It made books cheaper but introduced widespread typographical errors
It gave individuals and groups the ability to produce and distribute ideas without requiring approval from institutional gatekeepers
It created the first legal disputes over intellectual property and plagiarism
It allowed governments to standardize laws and enforce them uniformly across territories
questions.explanation: Before print, the Church and royal courts controlled manuscript copying and thus controlled which ideas circulated. The press broke that monopoly. Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517) spread across Germany within weeks — something impossible in the manuscript era. This democratization of publishing is the liberation side; censorship edicts and the Index of Forbidden Books are the control side.

questions.question: Which of the following best illustrates the 'control' side of the printing press revolution?
options: The rapid spread of Protestant pamphlets during the Reformation
The invention of the title page, allowing authors to be identified
Governments establishing licensing regimes requiring official approval before texts could be printed
The Catholic Church's decision to publish vernacular Bibles to counter Protestant influence
questions.explanation: Across Europe, governments and churches responded to the press by requiring printers to obtain licenses. England's Stationer's Company (1557) held a royal monopoly on printing, and works had to be approved before publication. France, the Holy Roman Empire, and others maintained similar systems. The same technology that spread the Reformation also handed authorities a new lever of censorship.

questions.question: What was the most ethically significant feature of the telegraph (and later the telephone) as an IT revolution?
options: It was the first technology to allow private, encrypted communication
It separated the speed of communication from the speed of physical transportation for the first time
It created a global infrastructure owned and operated by democratically accountable governments
It was the first electrical technology, transforming the energy economy alongside the information economy
questions.explanation: Before the telegraph (1837 onward), a message could travel no faster than a messenger. The telegraph sent information at the speed of electricity — transcontinental messages that would have taken weeks by horse now arrived in seconds. This was a qualitative break: for the first time, information and matter were decoupled. The ethical consequences (surveillance, market manipulation via early information asymmetry, military intelligence) followed from that basic fact.

questions.question: Radio and television introduced an ethical challenge that writing and print had not raised in the same form. What was it?
options: They were the first technologies capable of crossing national borders without physical interception
A tiny number of broadcasters could simultaneously reach millions of passive listeners, creating unprecedented potential for mass propaganda
They required expensive equipment, making them accessible only to wealthy individuals and governments
They were the first technologies to require real-time collaboration between producers and audiences
questions.explanation: Print is many-to-many: anyone with a press can publish. Broadcasting is one-to-many: a radio or TV transmitter reaches millions who cannot easily talk back. This asymmetry — a small elite controlling what millions hear and see — is what made mass media a tool of modern propaganda. Nazi Germany's use of radio, and later television's role in shaping political opinion, are the paradigm cases.

questions.question: ARPANET, the direct precursor to the modern internet, was originally developed under the sponsorship of:
options: IBM, as a commercial network for corporate data exchange
A consortium of American universities to share library resources
The U.S. Department of Defense, seeking a decentralized communication network resilient to attack
Tim Berners-Lee and colleagues at CERN, to share physics research
questions.explanation: ARPANET was funded by DARPA (the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency) and went live in 1969. The military motivation was to build a network with no single point of failure — if a node was destroyed, traffic would reroute. Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web (HTTP and HTML) in 1989–1991 at CERN, building on top of the internet infrastructure ARPANET had established.

questions.question: When the chapter examines how optimists and pessimists have responded to each IT revolution, which conclusion does it reach?
options: Optimists were ultimately right — each revolution expanded freedom more than it enabled oppression
Pessimists were ultimately right — established powers always successfully captured and controlled new technologies
Both were partly right: each revolution genuinely expanded freedom and genuinely enabled new forms of control
Neither was right — the effects of IT revolutions are too complex and contingent to generalize
questions.explanation: The chapter does not side with either the utopian or the dystopian view. The printing press really did spread the Reformation and democratize knowledge (optimists were right). It also really did give rise to systematic state censorship (pessimists were right). The same pattern repeats: the internet really did enable unprecedented free expression AND unprecedented surveillance. Both promises were kept.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why studying IT history is valuable for IT ethics?
options: History lets us predict precisely which technologies will be beneficial and which will be harmful
IT is unique among human activities because its ethical problems have no historical precedents
Recurring historical patterns reveal that how we design, govern, and contest technology profoundly shapes freedom, power, and justice
IT history shows that ethical problems are always solved eventually by market competition and technical progress
questions.explanation: The point is not that history repeats mechanically, but that it reveals patterns. Every generation has faced questions about who controls information, who is surveilled, whose voices are amplified and whose are silenced. Recognizing these patterns helps us ask sharper questions about AI, surveillance capitalism, and platform power today — rather than treating each new technology as entirely unprecedented.
